title: I'm Learning Drums. So Naturally I Wrote the Software.
excerpt: What starts as picking up drumsticks usually ends with evaluating MIDI libraries for .NET. At least for me.
body:
I've been learning to drum.

I realize that sentence pairs oddly with a blog post that's about to get into MIDI device polling and audio synthesis fallbacks, but here we are. I picked up drumming a while back and immediately ran into the classic beginner problem: I was getting louder but not necessarily *better*. Specifically, my timing was inconsistent in ways that are hard to hear in the moment but brutal to listen back to.

There are apps for this. Several, actually. Most of them are mobile-only, a few have subscription tiers for features that should just exist, and one presented me with so many onboarding modals that I closed it before I ever hit a drum. So I did what developers do: I built my own.

The result is [Rimshot](https://github.com/pstricker/rimshot), a drum timing trainer for .NET 8. The tagline is *"get better, not just louder,"* which I wrote for myself as much as anyone.

## What It Is

Rimshot is a rhythm game-style trainer. Notes scroll from right to left across the screen, and your job is to hit the correct drum pad, or keyboard key, at the right moment. The hit window is ±150ms: tight enough to matter, forgiving enough that you're not penalized for normal human reaction variation.

On a good hit you get a flash of lime green. Off the beat, you get the lane's color. It's a small thing but it works, your brain learns to chase the green.

The app supports a full 8-lane drum kit:

- Hi-hat
- Crash
- Snare
- Three toms
- Bass drum
- Floor tom
- Ride

If you have a USB MIDI drum kit, Rimshot auto-detects it on startup and reads velocity too, so harder hits feel harder. If you don't have a kit, or you're testing at 11pm and don't want to wake anyone up, there's a full keyboard fallback: `A S D F G Space J K` map to the eight lanes.

BPM is adjustable from 40 to 200 with a live re-sync when you change it, so you don't have to restart the loop. The metronome supports subdivisions at 1/1, 1/2, 1/4, and 1/8, useful when you're working on a specific subdivision feel rather than just quarter notes. There's also a Monitor tab that logs every hit with a timestamp and velocity reading, which I use to look at my consistency across a phrase. The monitor tab tells no lies.

## Why .NET?

Honest answer: I build .NET and cloud systems for a living. C# is the language I reach for when I want to move fast on a side project without fighting the tooling. That's most of the answer.

The other part is genuine curiosity. I'd never tried to do anything real-time with audio or MIDI in .NET. I knew the ecosystem existed but I didn't know how good it was. The dirty secret of side projects is that half the point is the excuse to learn something you haven't touched in your day job. Rimshot gave me a reason to find out if .NET could handle a drum trainer without major pain.

It can. More on that in a minute.

## The Technical Interesting Parts

### MIDI: DryWetMidi

For MIDI input I landed on [Melanchall.DryWetMidi](https://melanchall.github.io/drywetmidi/), which is the best-maintained MIDI library in the .NET ecosystem by a wide margin. The API is clean and the docs are actually good.

Auto-detecting the drum kit on startup looks roughly like this:

```csharp
var devices = InputDevice.GetAll();
var drumKit = devices.FirstOrDefault(d =>
    d.Name.Contains("drum", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase) ||
    d.Name.Contains("midi", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase));

if (drumKit != null)
{
    drumKit.EventReceived += OnMidiEventReceived;
    drumKit.StartEventsListening();
}
```

MIDI input is event-driven, you subscribe to `EventReceived`, and when the user hits a pad, you get a `NoteOnEvent` with a note number and velocity. The note number maps to which drum was hit; velocity is 0–127 and tells you how hard.

One thing I had to handle: key repeat suppression. On keyboards, holding a key fires repeated events at the OS repeat rate. On a real drum kit that's not a problem (you lift the stick), but in keyboard fallback mode it would register phantom hits. The fix is a set of currently-held keys and ignoring repeat events while the key is down.

```csharp
private readonly HashSet<Key> _heldKeys = new();

private void OnKeyDown(object sender, KeyEventArgs e)
{
    if (_heldKeys.Contains(e.Key)) return; // suppress repeat
    _heldKeys.Add(e.Key);
    ProcessHit(KeyToLane(e.Key));
}

private void OnKeyUp(object sender, KeyEventArgs e)
{
    _heldKeys.Remove(e.Key);
}
```

Simple, but easy to miss on the first pass.

### Audio: Silk.NET.OpenAL

For audio playback I used [Silk.NET.OpenAL](https://github.com/dotnet/Silk.NET), which is a managed wrapper around the OpenAL audio API. The pitch I made to myself was cross-platform from the start, I didn't want to be stuck on Windows if I ever wanted to run this on a Mac or Linux box.

Each lane loads a WAV sample file on startup and buffers it in OpenAL. When a hit fires, it plays the sample. Simple enough.

The interesting part is the fallback: if a WAV file isn't found for a lane, Rimshot synthesizes a basic percussive sound procedurally rather than silently failing. It's not a convincing snare drum, but it's better than silence when you're just testing the app without drum samples loaded. Procedural synthesis for percussion is mostly "generate a short noise burst, apply a fast exponential decay", not exactly music production, but functional.

### UI: Avalonia

I used [Avalonia](https://avaloniaui.net/) for the UI. If you're a WPF person, Avalonia is the cross-platform successor that didn't need to be rewritten, it's XAML-based, data-binding works the way you expect, and it runs on Windows, Mac, and Linux without a separate UI tier.

If you've looked at the repository and noticed it shows 43.9% HTML, that's Avalonia's AXAML markup files. They're XML/HTML-like in structure, hence the language detection.

The scrolling note lanes, hit rings, and monitor tab are all Avalonia controls. The hit feedback animations, expanding rings on pad hit, use Avalonia's animation system, which is more capable than I expected for something this niche.

Avalonia's learning curve is real if you're coming from WPF or WinForms. The data binding model is similar but not identical, and the community is smaller so some things that would have a StackOverflow answer for WPF don't have one for Avalonia. I ran into a couple of layout gotchas. But for a new cross-platform .NET desktop app in 2026, it's the right call.

### Timing

The ±150ms hit window came from experimenting. Too tight and it stops feeling like a trainer and starts feeling punitive. Too loose and you're not actually learning anything about your timing. 150ms landed at a place where a clean hit registers correctly and a sloppy one doesn't, which is what I wanted.

BPM live re-sync was important because the original version required stopping the loop to change tempo. That's annoying when you're warming up at 80 BPM and want to gradually push it to 120 without breaking your rhythm. The loop now recalculates note timing on the fly when BPM changes.

## Honest Takes

**.NET MIDI is more capable than I expected.** DryWetMidi is genuinely good. I went in ready to hit walls and mostly didn't. If you've been assuming .NET is a bad place for audio/MIDI work because it's a "business platform," check again.

**Avalonia is impressive but it's not WPF yet.** The fundamentals are solid and the cross-platform story is real. But the ecosystem maturity gap shows up in edge cases. Worth it for new projects; I'd be cautious about porting an existing WPF app.

**The monitor tab is the most useful thing in the app.** I added it because I thought logging hits might be interesting. Turns out watching your timestamps tells you a lot about your tendencies, whether you rush the beat, drag it, or crater on a specific subdivision. It's humbling.

**Side projects that scratch a real itch are the best kind.** I use Rimshot when I practice. That's not true of most side projects I've built. Having an actual user (me, trying to not embarrass myself at a drum kit) made every design decision more grounded.

## Give It a Try

The repo is at [github.com/pstricker/rimshot](https://github.com/pstricker/rimshot). You need the .NET 8 SDK to build and run it. If you have a USB MIDI kit, plug it in before launching, it'll auto-detect. If you don't, the keyboard fallback works fine for kicking the tires.

And if you're also learning drums and have strong opinions about hit windows or metronome subdivision models, the issues tab is open.
